sentences.text: And then, with these papers, I would be able to get, get around quite well.
I had a sidearm.
I had the whole uniform.
I had my birth certificate and then everything with me.
And I would be able to go around in Budapest quite well, no, no hindrance.

Even some of the Hungarian-- by the way, I forgot to mention, I think around September the 15th, very soon after, the-- that Lakatos government was overthrown by a Nazi quiddling again, a very-- they were mainly the, the Arrow Cross-- the most vicious Nazis that they could have.
At that time, they were already shooting people on the street.
If they caught you, you were a Jew-- and so we really intervened in many cases because we were the-- as Hungarian soldiers, we were a group of three people.
One of them was actually from, I think, from Kolozsvar.
His name was Loevy.
But his mother was an ethnic German.
And he was very blond and really, the most Aryan-looking individual you could ever see.
And there was another guy whose name I somehow can't remember now.
That's one of the things that I don't remember.

But we, as a group, were going around Budapest, and wherever we saw trouble with these Arrow Cross-- they were a bunch of hooligans that wound up with machine guns and, and hand guns, brand new ones, from the German arsenal that arrived to Budapest.
And their purpose in life was only one thing-- to kill.
And kill-- mainly kill Jews.
Sometimes by mistake, they killed their own, because they would accuse some people that you are a Jew, and shoot them on place without asking.
And they wound up to be not Jewish.
It happened many cases, many times.
Anyway, we were going around.
And we saved-- I believe we saved quite a few people this way.
And we put them into the safe houses.
That's the time when, if you remember, Wallenberg opened up the safe houses.
For the Swedish safe houses, I had 40, 50 certificates in the place that I slept.
Of course, not with me.
Or I would-- they would tell me, like I told you before, is that they took away such-and-such people by such-and-such a name, and the certificates would be made out to their name.
And I would go, as a Hungarian officer, go into where they kept them.
There was a brick factory they kept these people to be shipped, either to Auschwitz or, or to-- actually, they walked them to-- towards Vienna.
And I would go and take them out with these Schutz-Passes.
They were already [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] made for us.
As a matter of fact, I, I, I remember meeting Wallenberg himself-- on a few occasions, actually.
And we used these Schutz-Passes for quite-- a couple of weeks.
But then, I was called in.
And I was told that I have to pick up something in, in, in, I believe, [NON-ENGLISH].
And-- but I should not go.
They made me brand new papers then.
I should go as a Hungarian refugee, who is running away from the area in my hometown.
And again, they gave me genuine birth certificate.
They also gave me a certificate then that I was medically circumcised, although that you don't show unless they ask-- they, they take your pants down.
Anyway, we went to this place by the name Kecskemet.
Bilitzer instructed us that when we get to [NON-ENGLISH], to go to the Jewish community, and just a certain password.
And if we say that password, we will receive a package and then bring it back.
The other two that went with me was supposed to remain there.
However, we wound up in Kecskemet.
And then we took an-- if I'm not mistaken-- a narrow-gauge railroad to a town by the name Gyula.
And from there, you can see already the mountains start.
And that's where the Hungarian-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] previously, you know, the Hungarian-Transylvanian border was.
And we, we were instructed to go-- a certain farmer-- a, a Hungarian farmer would meet us at a certain place, who was from the other side of the border, and will take us across the mountain.
And after we left just the first upgrade, we were walking, and we ran into a German patrol.
And they caught us and took us in, and, and started beating us with the leg of a chair.
And I kept on saying, hey, I am this and that.
They, they never took my pants down, you know.
But they-- after about an hour, or an hour-and-a-half interrogation, they just let me go.
They said, don't associate with Jewish people.
Do you know that you were together with Jewish people?
I said to them, no, come on, now.
He's German.
I know that.
They just let me go.
And I don't know whatever happened to the two other people.
I wound up in-- across the border because of-- the, the, the, the Hungarian peasant.
They let him go immediately, because he said, I have nothing to do with these people.
I just met them here.
He says, I'm a farmer from here.
And they didn't even take him in.
They just took us in.
Anyway, I, I met up with him and crossed the border.
And somehow, willingly or unwillingly, he took, took me into Romanian border guards.
I'll never forget that-- the huge weapons-- long rifles, maybe 6 foot tall.
I never saw rifles like that, with bayonets.
And soldiers in, in green uniform, with-- not shoes-- they had from leather.
They called it in Hungarian, [NON-ENGLISH].
I was-- both of them-- they didn't-- the army-- the Romanian Army probably didn't give them shoes.
They, they wound up in these native tires, whatever, for shoes.
And, and I kept on telling it, try to talk Hungarian to them.
But they did not speak Hungarian.
And they just tied me up with a rope-- and, and both of them, with the guns.
And we were very close to, to-- I, I believe it was [NON-ENGLISH] because I saw it.
It was just by the outskirts of the city.
He took me into a police jail, not even a station.
And there were little booths, like guard booths.
And that, that was the prison.
They put you into the guard booth and locked you in there.
However, when they caught me, they already know-- knew that was money.
I was money to them.
And they went.
And a Jewish guy from the community of the city came over.
And he spoke to them in Romanian.
And they didn't let me go.
However, they laid me down in the booth.
The whole booth, they laid down.
And they untied my hands so I will be able, sort of, to sleep there in the night.
And in the morning, they took me, and took me into the Jewish community.
And I met this guy by the name Zieman.
And he gave me that package.
And now the thing was to go across the border.
But to go across the border, I sort of took-- there, there was a paper bag that I knew there was money in.
And I tied it like a belt to my body, with a-- with a-- like a tallis.
You know what a tallis is?
A thing-- and I tied it there.
And then, on both sides, what were-- they were watches.
So I took them out of the boxes.
They were Swiss watches-- Omega, and very, very-- and I had, I think, about 15 or 20 of them.
And I tied them on both sides.
And didn't need any water-crossing garb, because I knew that whatever they knew, I know already.
I went across.
And I took that same thing from Gyula.
And by that time, I, I had my Hungarian papers hidden-- I forgot to tell you.
I had it hidden on that side of the border.
I picked it up, and, and, and also a shirt I had hidden in the same place.
I took that shirt.
Then I took the train back to Budapest.
And I brought back the money to Bilitzer.
And this took about seven or eight days, the whole thing.
And in the seven or eight days, when I got back to Budapest, it was terrible already.
Oh, it was awful.
They, they, they were taking Jews, shooting them.
And our-- the situation became awful.
And--
This is also 1944.
And this had to be, already, in end of October, November-- in November.
And the, the situation for the Jewish people became awful.
They, they shot anything and anybody on the street.
Suspecting being Jewish, or not Jewish-- they, they were just hoodlums.
The ones I left, but now they had full reign.
Because they were organized.
They were-- and, and-- anyway--
Who were shooting?
These Arrow--
First of all, we saw the Gestapo with the chains, walking around with these Arrow Cross people.
And they had one thing in their mind only-- killing.
That's all they did.
They took people to the Danube.
They shot them in.
I watched it more than once.
They took groups and tied them up, and-- just like you saw in film.
Not quite like that, but pretty close to that, I've seen them where they machine-gunned them and tossed them right over the bridge into the water.
Women, children together, doesn't make no difference to them.
Anyway, we were this time moved to outside of Budapest, in a house that we rented.
Well, you can call that what-- we-- at that time, I didn't know I was a partisan.
But the activities we did was like it.
Because we didn't go sabotaging anything.
We just went to save.
Our purpose was only to save people-- as many as we could, and bring them into the Schutz Houses.
Yeah.
They, they called it Brihah.
I don't know if it means anything.
It's a Hebrew name, meaning to run, run away, OK?
No, no, no partisan-- we didn't fight.
In essence, we did fight sometimes.
We-- when we-- but that came a little later.
At this time, we moved there.
And we had weapons.
We had-- you would be able to buy from the SS-- Schmeissers.
Only-- apparently, the money that I bought back gave us the ability to buy weapons.
And I don't know how much, but I think it was like $10,000.
It was not a large amount, but it was large enough.
Because we would be able to buy for $20 a Schmeisser.
I bought them myself from SS-- genuine SS troopers.
And once in a while, they would even come in to the Schutz House-- the Swiss Schutz House.
I brought them in to this Bilitzer.
And we made deals, like, 10, 15 Schmeissers we bought from them.
We bought from them also uniforms.
You could buy anything you wanted from the Germans.
The only thing they wanted is, when, when it's over, you remember me.
I'm the guy who helped you.
And by the way, these were the same Einsatzgruppen who did all the murdering in, in, in Poland and all over.
But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were in this building.
We rented this in Kobanya, they called it, in, in Budapest, one of the outskirts.
It was totally away from the center where the ghetto was.
And we were coming and going there, like, like, you know, like refugees from the east who ran away.
Because the Russians were already in the-- you know, like, like, four or five days after I left the city there, the Russians occupied it.
Just that close-- they, they overran.
And, and somehow, they broke through the fronts.
And they just swept Romania-- through.
And that, that came in, in a day or two.
Like, like, when I was there, they were saying that they'll take another month or two.
When I left, three days later, there were the Russians there already.
So if I would have stayed, I probably would have been liberated.
But that's-- that was not my mission.
So I came back.
And as I said, we used whatever I brought-- the funds-- to save people, mainly.
And we succeeded.
Till I told you that I was in this apartment.
And one of the Polish Jewish people who was with us-- who we don't know to today whether he was a genuine refugee or he was a Pole who worked with the Gestapo.
We really don't know.
We, we suspect that he was one of them.
Because he took one of the weapons and started firing into the ceiling in a-- in a home that you live in.
And, of course, they called the Gestapo.
And they came.
And they arrested us.
And they took us up to Buda, to the Schwab head, beat us up.
By that time, I had no-- you know, I hid the papers.
We flushed them down the toilet.
And we were just Jews.
And we also took the weapons.
And they had a, like, a hot-water heater.
And we broke the top open.
And we threw them in there. we had a choice, either to fight them-- we were about eight people there.
And they had about 200 of them.
And the question was, do we go down with them, or try to save us?
And the thing was that, first, the guy who fired into the ceiling disappeared.
This is why we think he might have-- with them.
And then when they came in, they were looking for the weapons all over.
Because that would-- that-- if they would have found the weapons, they were entitled to shoot us on the spot.
They were entitled to do it anyway.
But that would have given them more of an excuse.
So, as I said, is-- we, we took these weapons and put them into the water tank.
And they looked all over.
But we somehow cleaned up the top that we broke in.
That was a different-- it was not a hot-water heater.
It was more of a water tank, where the water would come from.
And then it would get heated outside.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took us up to Buda.
And there, they were beating us.
And I don't know what happened to the other people.
But what happened to me is that, I had never, even in my hometown in the ghetto, seen people beaten up so savagely.
There, there, there were noses missing.
There were eyes knocked out-- unbelievable.
And the ladies were in one place.
And the men were on the other place.
In the bottom, there was a little road.
And after I got interrogated the first time-- and I was beaten, I told you, with this chair leg-- they threw me in there.
And then-- I was one of the youngest.
So one of the guards grabbed me.
Because we had-- on the men's side, they cooked soup, what they call is the [NON-ENGLISH] soup.
You know what [NON-ENGLISH] soup is?
The caraway seed soup, with some flour.
And we brought it to the women.
So two of us-- me and another guy, and the guard in back of me-- carried the big kettle of soup over to the other side.
And I-- when I was carrying it, I was looking, nah, this is the time to get.
And on the way back, I, I, I took the pot, threw it over the guard, and started running.
And the other guy ran the other way.
He fired at me first.
But I was already over the hill.
And I don't know what happened to the other guy.
I just-- I don't know.
And I hid.
And again, I jumped on a-- the bridges.
This was some time already in the end of November, beginning of December, when the Russians were already bombarding heavily.
And some of the bridges were bombed and blow-- blew up.
So there was only one bridge to go across from Buda to, to, to Pecs.
And I waited.
And at night, I jumped on one of the trucks with a canvas on, covered myself.
And I was hoping-- and I heard the guard on the other side-- OK, go.
So I got off, jumped off, and actually walked.
But at that time, Budapest was already bombarded all the way, you know, by the Russians.
They must have been very, very close.
And I went back to, again, to the Schutz House, to the Swiss house, where this Bilitzer was.
And he gave me papers as an SS trooper, Volksdeutscher.
And, and for the three weeks, I was an SS trooper.
And, and I did the same thing with two other SS guys, who were also Jewish.
We went on the street.
And in that case, we were trying to catch these Arrow Guards.
And-- but we were afraid that they themselves might be Jewish.
We never knew.
So this is when we did the job of getting their pants down.
And if he-- because these were only murderers.
If we caught one of them, we shot him.
If he was the genuine article, we, we got rid of him-- got rid of quite a few of them.
And this went on and on till, I remember, Christmas, we were still down-- I went back to the Schutz House in my uniform-- on one end.
But chaos was really going on then.
Nobody checked on anybody.
It was a free-for-all.
But they still-- if they caught Jews, they would shoot them.
They-- however, they stayed away from the Swedish and the-- and the Swiss-protected homes of the Schutz Houses.
The reason for it all-- because they were paid off.
The police, the SS, they were all paid off.
Not only they were paid off, because the hierarchy knew the fate-- what's going to happen to them.
And I remember the last time.
Still, they had, like, like-- a couple of days before Christmas, I went with this girl, and, and we were sitting in a cafe in the, the-- we were trying to plan where to go.
Because we, we had some homes that they picked up some Jews from the ghetto already.
And they were trying to take them away.
And just then, a Gestapo patrol came, you know, with the chains.
And, you know, they wanted Papieren.
And, and I gave them my Papieren.
They were quite satisfied.
But they were not satisfied with the-- don't forget, I spoke German now, really.
But they were not satisfied with my companions.
They kept on saying, Du bist ein Juden.
And I said, no.
She really didn't look Jewish.
And I said, why would she be here, I said, with a German soldier, you know?
No way.
Anyway, they, they took her away.
But they let her go.
So she came back to the house.
And again, this routine patrol we did till about two or three days before the Russians came in.
Then the bombardment was so intense that you couldn't do anything.
They couldn't do anything, either.
And there were corpses all over, littered there, maybe tens of thousands of bodies in the street, just thrown all over.
And one morning, I decided that we had to go put my uniform on and go look what's happening.
But in the meantime, I stuck my head out from the Swiss Schutz House, where I stayed in the basement, and I see the Russian soldiers walking around.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [CRYING] so happened again, is I started going-- I went down, yelling, hey, the Russians are here.
And of course, the Russians were not that interested in Jews or non-Jews.
They were also-- they, they-- guns.
And I thought-- I knew the two words he yelled at me-- [NON-ENGLISH].
And I said, well, I don't give a shit whether you're-- you'll excuse the expression-- whether you're Jewish or not.
They just kept on going.
These are the front troops.
They were the first guard that came in.
They were unbelievable collection of, of riffraff with machine guns.
And-- but they did the fighting.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, of course, that day, I went down.
And I brought everybody up.
And we stayed there.
And then I told them that first thing I want to do is go home to see if there are any remnants of my family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was leaving Budapest, like, two days after the liberation.
I remember, for the first two days, I stayed there.
Not only that, I found out that some of the men from my hometown who were older than me-- they were in the labor camps-- were captured by the Russians, and that they joined the Russian army.
So quite a few of them were in the main troops that took in-- they were in Budapest.
And I met one of them.
And when I asked him, what's happening in our hometown, he says, well, you know, I was there when our-- your-- our hometown was liberated the first day.
But they were liberated by the Czech army, not by the Russians.
So I was-- just sort of went through.
I was there a half a day.
And at that time, everything looked as normal as, as could be.
The only thing is-- without Jews.
Couldn't believe it, he says.
There's nobody there.
And he told me of one individual that survived, who was also hiding between two buildings, and survived the war because he had outside help.
You see, we didn't have outside help.
That was one of our mistakes.
However, I don't know, in our case, how the outside help would have worked.
In his case, the neighbor was the one.
They were between two buildings.
There was a, a building area.
And he was there.
And they supplied him with food and water.
I don't know how they could have done it in my case.
Because in my case, the German headquarters were above us.
So anyway, I survived, and so did the other people survive-- also in a Schutz House, I don't know whether the Swedish one or the Swiss one.
And then, two or three days, I told you before, I started going home in a overnight train that was going back to Russia to pick up-- pick up-- I don't know, whatever.
It was an empty train.
And it was terribly, terribly cold.
It was an awfully cold winter.
And I was clothed very lightly.
And I was in this freight wagon all by myself, with a little straw on it.
And I covered myself with straw.
And then we got into one of the cities.
I don't even remember which one it was.
And a Russian patrol checked the train.
And they found me there and took me off, took me to the headquarters and interrogated me for about 24 hours.
And I kept on saying, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], I'm a Jew.
And they-- he couldn't care less.
He said-- 'capitalist' and 'espionage,' he was talking.
Because Russian, I didn't speak it at that time.
That's all the words I heard from him.
But he lived with a Hungarian woman.
And I think he used her as a girlfriend.
And she got friendly with him.
And, and she kept on putting her hand around me, and said-- I remember the words in Russian-- [NON-ENGLISH], yeah [NON-ENGLISH].
And just-- she said, OK, get lost.
And then I stayed with this woman in a different room.
And she told me, look, don't stay too long, because this guy might get jealous again.
You never know.
And next morning, she put me on his [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] truck.
He had a truck.
And they took me to Debrecen-- all the way.
And in Debrecen, I spent a day in the Jewish community.
There were already Jewish people coming back.
They were mainly men who were in the labor camp.
There were no women at all.
And from there, I wound up in my hometown.
And I came back.
And it was devastating.
There was nobody left.
There were only a couple of Jewish people who were in the Czech Army, or who were-- who were formerly in the labor service.

And also some of them who were in the Russian Army, were running things there, because-- and, and also the local [? roussiens, ?] who were the other side of the city-- the periphery of the city, they lived.
They also were in charge of the running the city because the Czechs were there at the time.
But then soon after, the Czechs got kicked out.
Because Stalin decided to, to annex that part to Russia.
And he, at that time, already started to get rid of the Czech soldiers.
Although they were still there, but he was putting in slowly.
That was the time-- the period that I was there, is when the Czech soldiers were being replaced.
The Czech police, the Czech Army, the Czech city hall representative were being replaced with the Russians.
And I was there and didn't get a very good reception, even from the local Jewish people, because they were telling me they're communist.
And did I have any property?
It belongs to them.
And I should give it to them.
And I don't want to go into it.
The guy who really was after me the mostly is a multimillionaire in this city now.
And he didn't seem to be that much of a communist.
He was a communist only for the day against me, but not against himself.
And--
Well, I wanted to stay in my hometown till the war would wind up to see if anybody or any members came back.
But as I said, is-- I got involved with this man who joined the militia, he called it-- also got a gun.
And he knew that my father had gold.
And he wanted to get it as badly as he can.
And all of a sudden, he's beating me up-- what did I do with the gold?
And we did have some.
And we did hide some.
But I didn't take it because I didn't want him to have it.
And he-- when he beat me up, I said, now, this I need?
So I took off.
And I went by train to Bucharest.
And in Bucharest, I met up with this Zieman, who-- then, I knew who he was.
In-- when, when I was in, in the city, in, in-- I believe it was [NON-ENGLISH].
I never really met him.
And I just knew there was somebody.
Supposedly, he was dropped off by parachute at the same time.
I learned that after.
Because they didn't want to give me that information.
And they didn't even want me to meet with this individual who brought-- excuse me.
